corpus.title: News: Framingham Sleep Cohort Study — Accurate Report
corpus.snippet: A 10-year longitudinal cohort study found that adults who slept fewer than 6 hours per night had a 1...
corpus.content:
Breaking: Framingham Sleep Cohort Study makes a significant move amid changes in the scientific sector

A 10-year longitudinal cohort study found that adults who slept fewer than 6 hours per night had a 1.7-fold higher risk of developing hypertension compared to those sleeping 7-8 hours. According to sources familiar with the matter, the decision follows months of internal deliberation. Industry analysts noted the move signals a broader shift in scientific strategy. A spokesperson for Framingham Sleep Cohort Study declined to comment beyond the official statement.

corpus.title: Sleep Duration and Incident Hypertension: 10-Year Follow-Up of the Framingham Sleep Cohort
corpus.snippet: A 10-year longitudinal cohort study found that adults who slept fewer than 6 hours per night had a 1.7-fold higher risk ...
corpus.content:
ORIGINAL RESEARCH — Circulation
Published: February 6, 2024 | DOI: 10.1161/CIRCULATIONAHA.123.066714

STUDY DESIGN: Prospective longitudinal cohort. n=4,810 adults aged 30-65, free of hypertension at enrolment (2010-2013). Follow-up duration: 10 years (through 2022-2023). Sleep duration assessed by wrist actigraphy at baseline and at 5-year follow-up.

PRIMARY OUTCOME: Incident hypertension (SBP ≥130 or DBP ≥80 mmHg, or antihypertensive medication initiation).

RESULTS:
Short sleep (<6 hours/night): hazard ratio (HR) for incident hypertension = 1.73 (95% CI: 1.48-2.02), adjusted for age, sex, BMI, smoking, alcohol, physical activity, and baseline blood pressure.
Reference group: 7-8 hours/night.
Long sleep (>9 hours/night): HR = 1.29 (95% CI: 1.04-1.60).
10-year cumulative incidence of hypertension: 38.4% in short sleepers vs 22.6% in reference group.

CONCLUSION: Short sleep duration is independently associated with a 1.7-fold elevated risk of incident hypertension. Clinical screening for sleep duration should be integrated into cardiovascular risk assessment.
Framingham Sleep Cohort Study, Boston University School of Medicine.

Framingham Sleep Cohort Study ANNOUNCES CONTINUED SCIENTIFIC CONTROVERSY

Multiple sources now confirm: A 10-year longitudinal cohort study found that adults who slept fewer than 6 hours per night had a 1.7-fold higher risk of developing hypertension compared to those sleeping 7-8 hours. The announcement comes as reports circulating about scientific. Observers say this could have significant implications for scientific stakeholders. The full impact is expected to become clear over the coming months.
